10 Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacífico S.A.B. de C.V (PAC)

3.25%

MXF’s holdings are not overly concentrated in any one sector, with only beverages and financial groups making up greater than 10% of NAV (13.60% and 11.19% respectively). Additional holdings consist of airports, auto parts, building materials, chemical products, consumer products, food, mining, railroads, real estate, and restaurants. Due to the funds’ diversification consisting of many sectors of the Mexican economy, and the modest GDP expectations for 2026, I feel comfortable maintaining my ‘Hold’ position.

Additionally, when we take a more concentrated look at the fund’s major holdings, analysts maintain mixed projections. For example, Grupo México (OTCPK:GMBXF), Grupo Financiero Banorte (OTCQX:GBOOY), and Fomento Económico Mexicano (FMX) maintain analyst ratings ranging from ‘Hold’ to ‘Strong Buy’.

When looking at recent top performers like Cemex, Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings currently show it as a ‘Strong Buy’, with positive valuation, growth, and profitability metrics. However, Cemex has remained highly cyclical over the last 10 years. With the stock currently trading at all-time highs, up over 90% YTD, I believe that it is not unforeseeable that changing economic conditions could lead to a temporary pullback. With Cemex making up nearly 5% of the fund’s holdings, such a pullback could hold back future returns.

Valuation

Since MXF operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a discount or premium to the actual value of the underlying assets. Earlier this year, the fund’s discount to NAV reached 23.18%, having since recovered to 14.76%. Today’s discount is in line with the 52-week average discount to NAV of 17.59%.

While the current discount does provide a modest undervaluation compared to the value of the underlying assets, taken into consideration with the historical discount, it is my opinion that this undervaluation does not provide a compelling enough reason for opening/ adding to a position.

Peer Comparison

MXF has outperformed its peers during the last year, returning 44.90%. By comparison, the Mexico Equity & Income Fund (MXE) performed similarly, returning 40.83%, albeit with a higher expense ratio (1.51% compared to MXF’s 1.03%). Additionally, MXF significantly outperformed other peers in its peer profile. including Taiwan Fund, Inc. (TWN), Hartford Capital Appreciation Fund (HHCAX), Fidelity Advisor Healthcare Fund (HHCCX), and NYLI MacKay DefinedTerm Municipal Opportunities Fund (MMD). It's important to note the rather obvious differences in their focus, however, as their holdings reflect different holdings.

In terms of expense ratios, MXF falls somewhat in the middle when compared to its peers. However, when looking at other alternatives to gain Mexican exposure in one’s portfolio, there are seemingly cheaper options. For example, iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW) offers an expense ratio of 0.50%, and has also slightly outperformed MXF on both a YTD and 12-month time frame.

Economic Considerations

Based on the most recent outlook from the Banco de México, the central bank foresees real GDP growth in 2026 of approximately 1.1% (with an expected range between 0.3% to 1.9%). In contrast, The International Monetary Fund has a slightly more optimistic base case scenario, estimating growth at 1.5% for 2026. While growth is expected to continue at a conservative rate, it does present an opportunity cost problem, where perhaps greater returns could be achieved in regions experiencing higher rates of growth.

For inflation, data from October 2025 shows inflation currently at 3.57%, representing a 19 basis point decrease from the prior month. While inflation continues to move in the right direction, with the central bank seeing a continued move towards 3% by mid/end of 2026, some analysts warn that inflation could still remain a persistent issue throughout the next year. For example, inflation in areas such as food, agriculture, and energy continues to remain uncertain, with these sectors being exposed to pressures from global commodity prices.

In the context of Mexico’s economic outlook, the peso remains stable, albeit down from levels seen earlier in the year. Currently, the peso is trading within a range of 18.30 to 18.35. Over the last 12 months this range has been between approximately 18.20 to 21.30 per USD, with the current exchange rate showing a pullback nearer to the bottom of the 12-month range.

Risks/Challenges

Similar to many emerging market funds, MXF is exposed to currency and exchange rate risk coupled with economic risk. Since the underlying holdings of the fund are in pesos, any economic fluctuation could have a negative result on the fund’s performance. Additionally, as a closed-end fund, it is typical for MXF to trade below NAV. While a discount to NAV can often be seen as an undervaluation and a mismatch of purchase price to underlying assets, this discount could widen in adverse market conditions.

A widening of the NAV discount or a depreciation of the peso could ultimately hamper underlying NAV growth. Additionally, as the Mexican economy is correlated to the US, rate volatility could also cause drawdowns. Persistent volatility or regulatory changes could pressure dividends, which would have a negative effect on the appeal of the fund, that being the yield + discount. All in all, given the fund’s performance over the last year, even if the Mexican economy does moderately well throughout the next year, that is no guarantee that the fund will continue to outperform. Political, regulatory, and currency risk will always remain a factor.

According to the company’s most recent semi-annual report, the fund also identifies current global conflicts such as the Israel-Hammas conflict and special military operation in Ukraine as potential risks, resulting in increased volatility to global financial markets and commodity prices. The fund also identifies the uncertain US tariff policy as a potential future risk.

Finally, it is worth noting that MXF remains between the overbought and oversold zone according to the RSI indicator. The current RSI is 59, reflecting that despite the over 40% YTD returns, investors remain somewhat neutral. It is my view that, despite the varying economic forecasts for 2026, current fund holdings consist of well performing businesses that could perform well in an economy with muted growth expectations. This view is supported by current investor sentiment.

Take Away

MXF offers investors a way to gain Central American/Latin American exposure in their portfolios. During the past year, the fund has significantly outperformed the broad market and its peers. However, in light of stellar performance in 2025, and based on conservative economic estimates, and historical discount to NAV, I rate CEE a ‘Hold’. Investors should continue to monitor the economic conditions in the region, the peso exchange rate, and investment allocations of the fund. Additionally, given the fund’s expense ratio of 1.03%, other options with lower expense ratios should also be considered when allocating for Mexican exposure in one’s portfolio.

data.title: Boardwalk REIT: Canadian Residential Real Estate At A 35% Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Some of the Canadian residential REITs have gotten increasingly attractive in the past few months. Boardwalk REIT (BEI.UN:CA) (OTCPK:BOWFF) is one of those residential REITs, as despite its strong performance, the share price is currently trading approximately 15% lower than the share price when my previous article was published this summer.

A Strong AFFO Makes the Stock Even More Attractive Than This Summer

While a REIT should never be judged based on its net profit, it's usually the starting point of the FFO calculation, and that’s also the case at Boardwalk REIT. As you can see below, the REIT reported a net profit of C$37.6 million, and after adding back some non-cash items like the fair value decrease in the portfolio and after adding back the distributions on the Class B Units, the FFO came in at almost $66 million, which represents an increase of almost 10% compared to the FFO result in Q3 2024.

On a per-share basis, the improvement is even more visible. The FFO per share came in at C$1.23, which represents an 11% increase. That’s due to the lower total share count, which decreased by approximately 1% to 53.5 million units in the third quarter of 2025.

One of the main reasons why I like Canadian REITs is the fact that their maintenance capex is included in the AFFO calculation. As you can see below, the REIT spent approximately C$8.6 million of the FFO on maintenance capex, and this means the AFFO was C$57.4 million, or C$1.07 per share.

Looking at the 9M 2025 performance, the total AFFO came in at just under C$159 million, representing C$2.97 per share.

That’s a pretty strong Q3 (and subsequently 9M) performance, and this allowed Boardwalk to once again increase its full-year guidance. Whereas the REIT generated just C$3.56 per share in AFFO in 2024, we will now very likely see a 10%+ improvement. As you can see below, Boardwalk initially aimed for an AFFO of C$3.62-3.92 per share but hiked this to C$3.85-4.00 per share after reporting on its Q2 results.

And now, after a strong Q3 result, Boardwalk has further increased its full-year AFFO guidance. The midpoint of C$3.985 per share now represents a 12% increase compared to the AFFO per share in FY 2024 and another 1.5% increase compared to the full-year guidance after the Q2 results were published.

This also means the current distribution rhythm of C$1.62 per share per year (payable in monthly installments) represents a payout ratio of just over 40% of the anticipated AFFO this year. At the current share price, with the stock trading at just over 16 times AFFO and 14 times FFO, Boardwalk’s management is prioritizing stock buybacks. Boardwalk has spent C$31 million on stock buybacks in the first nine months of the year and has spent an additional C$6 million on buying back its own stock after the end of the quarter. This will further boost the per-share performance heading into 2026.

It also means Boardwalk is buying back these units at an attractive discount to the NAV. As you can see below, the total Net Asset Value came in at C$5.23 billion, representing just under C$98/share.

And considering the portfolio is valued at an average capitalization rate of 5.12% (with cap rates ranging from 4.57% in Ontario to 5.68% in Saskatchewan), the NAV/share is quite realistic. The sensitivity analysis below also indicates that for a 25 bps increase in the cap rate, the fair value of the assets decreases by C$378 million.

So even if I were to use a required cap rate of 5.75% in combination with a 1% NOI increase, the net impact on the NAV/share would be less than C$15, as the NAV/share would still come in above C$80 per unit.

Investment Thesis

While I didn’t immediately pull the trigger on initiating a long position in Boardwalk REIT after my previous article was published, I am now ready to do so. I have plenty of Canadian dollars burning a hole in my pocket after selling certain positions into the mining rally in the past few months, and rather than converting those dollars back into euros, I am looking to redeploy my excess amount of Canadian dollars.

Boardwalk REIT fits the bill. The stock is trading at just 16 times the AFFO, with additional AFFO increases to be expected (I now expect C$4.05 and C$4.15 in 2026 and 2027 AFFO per share, respectively), and I think the REIT’s plan and ability to buy back its own shares is a good and value-enhancing move. As the stock is trading at a 35% discount to its NAV, buying back its own shares likely is the best investment Boardwalk can make.

I currently have no position in Boardwalk REIT, but I may initiate a long position in the next few days or weeks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
